questions.specification: A rectangular coil with 120 turns, length $0.10\ \text{m}$ and width $0.05\ \text{m}$ is pulled at constant velocity $2.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ into a uniform magnetic field of $0.40\ \text{T}$ perpendicular to the plane of the coil. It takes $0.025\ \text{s}$ for the entire coil to enter the field.

parts.content: Calculate the change in magnetic flux linkage through the coil during this interval.
parts.markscheme:
Area $A = 0.10 \times 0.05 = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$
$\Delta \Phi = NBA = 120 \times 0.40 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.24\ \text{Wb}$
Correct calculation of area/substitution <marks>1</marks>
Correct result with units <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To calculate the change in magnetic flux linkage, we first need to identify the key values provided in the question:

*   Number of turns, $N = 120$
*   Length of the coil, $l = 0.10\ \text{m}$
*   Width of the coil, $w = 0.05\ \text{m}$
*   Magnetic field strength, $B = 0.40\ \text{T}$

The question also mentions that the coil is pulled into the field until it has entirely entered. This means we are calculating the change from zero flux (when it's outside) to the maximum flux (when it's fully inside).

steps.title: Calculate the coil area
steps.content:
First, we calculate the geometric area ($A$) of the rectangular coil. 

$$\begin{aligned} A &= \text{length} \times \text{width} \\ A &= 0.10\ \text{m} \times 0.05\ \text{m} \\ A &= 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the rectangular coil.

steps.title: Calculate the flux linkage
steps.content:
Magnetic flux linkage ($\Phi$) is the product of the number of turns, the magnetic field strength, and the area of the coil perpendicular to the field. Since the coil starts completely outside the field (initial flux $= 0$), the change in flux linkage is equal to the final flux linkage:

$$\Delta \Phi = NBA$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \Phi &= 120 \times 0.40\ \text{T} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2 \\ \Delta \Phi &= 0.24\ \text{Wb} \end{aligned}$$

Note that the units for magnetic flux linkage are Webers ($\text{Wb}$).

calculator.description: Calculate the total change in magnetic flux linkage.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The change in magnetic flux linkage is $0.24\ \text{Wb}$.

**Marking Breakdown:**
*   **1 Mark:** Correct calculation of the area ($5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$) or correct substitution into the formula.
*   **1 Mark:** Correct final result ($0.24$) with the appropriate unit ($\text{Wb}$).

steps.title: Identify the cutting length
steps.content:
To use the motional EMF approach, we first need to determine the length $l$ of the side of the coil that is perpendicular to the motion as it enters the field. 

We are told it takes $0.025\text{ s}$ to enter at a velocity of $2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. We can calculate the distance traveled during this time:
$$\text{distance} = v \times t = 2.0\text{ m s}^{-1} \times 0.025\text{ s} = 0.05\text{ m}$$

Since this distance corresponds to the width of the coil ($0.05\text{ m}$), the side perpendicular to the motion (the length "cutting" the field lines) must be the other dimension:
$$l = 0.10\text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.0 * 0.025 on the main screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance the coil travels to enter the field.

highlights.text: takes $0.025\text{ s}$ for the entire coil to enter

steps.title: Calculate the motional EMF
steps.content:
The motional EMF ($\varepsilon$) induced in a single wire moving through a magnetic field is given by $\varepsilon = Bvl$. Since the coil has $N = 120$ turns, the total EMF induced as the leading edge enters the field is:

$$\varepsilon = N B v l$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\varepsilon = 120 \times 0.40\text{ T} \times 2.0\text{ m s}^{-1} \times 0.10\text{ m}$$
$$\varepsilon = 9.6\text{ V}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total induced EMF.

steps.title: Find change in flux
steps.content:
According to Faraday's Law, the magnitude of the induced EMF is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$$

We can rearrange this to solve for the change in magnetic flux linkage ($\Delta \Phi$):
$$\Delta \Phi = \varepsilon \Delta t$$

Substitute the EMF we found and the time interval:
$$\Delta \Phi = 9.6\text{ V} \times 0.025\text{ s}$$
$$\Delta \Phi = 0.24\text{ Wb}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final change in magnetic flux linkage.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
The change in magnetic flux linkage is $0.24\text{ Wb}$. 

In the IB markscheme, you earn 1 mark for the correct substitution of values (calculating the total linkage) and 1 mark for the final result with correct units (Webers, $\text{Wb}$).

steps.title: Identify Faraday's Law
steps.content:
To find the average induced electromotive force (emf), we use **Faraday's Law of Induction**. This law states that the induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage. Since the coil enters the field from a region of zero flux, the change in flux linkage is the total flux linkage when the coil is fully inside the field.

This concept is part of **Topic D.4: Induction (HL only)**.

steps.title: Calculate the coil area
steps.content:
First, we calculate the area $A$ of the rectangular coil. The question provides the length and width of the coil:

$$\begin{aligned} A &= \text{length} \times \text{width} \\ A &= 0.10\ \text{m} \times 0.05\ \text{m} \\ A &= 0.005\ \text{m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the area of the rectangular coil.

steps.title: Calculate change in flux linkage
steps.content:
The magnetic flux linkage is given by $N\Phi = NBA$ (assuming the field is perpendicular to the plane of the coil, so $\cos(0) = 1$). Since the coil starts outside the field, the initial flux is zero. The change in flux linkage $\Delta(N\Phi)$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta(N\Phi) &= N \times B \times A \\ \Delta(N\Phi) &= 120 \times 0.40\ \text{T} \times 0.005\ \text{m}^2 \\ \Delta(N\Phi) &= 0.24\ \text{Wb (webers)} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the total change in magnetic flux linkage.

steps.title: Determine average induced emf
steps.content:
The average emf $\varepsilon$ is the change in flux linkage divided by the time interval $\Delta t$:

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= \frac{\Delta(N\Phi)}{\Delta t} \\ \varepsilon &= \frac{0.24\ \text{Wb}}{0.025\ \text{s}} \\ \varepsilon &= 9.6\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the average induced emf.

steps.title: Identify the motional emf formula
steps.content:
When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, an electromotive force (emf) is induced across it. For a rectangular coil with multiple turns, the total induced emf is given by the motional emf formula:

$$\varepsilon = NBLv$$

Where:
- $N$ is the number of turns
- $B$ is the magnetic field strength
- $L$ is the length of the side cutting the field lines
- $v$ is the velocity of the coil

steps.title: Determine the cutting length
steps.content:
We need to figure out which side of the coil ($0.10\text{ m}$ or $0.05\text{ m}$) is perpendicular to the motion and "cutting" the magnetic field lines. 

The question states it takes $0.025\text{ s}$ for the entire coil to enter at $2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$. We can calculate the distance traveled using the kinematic equation from **Topic A.1**:

$$\text{distance} = v \times t = 2.0 \times 0.025 = 0.05\text{ m}$$

Since the distance required to fully enter the field is $0.05\text{ m}$, this corresponds to the **width**. Therefore, the **length** ($0.10\text{ m}$) is the side cutting the field lines.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of velocity (2.0) and time (0.025).

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of velocity and time on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Multiply velocity by time to find the distance.

calculator.description: Verify the distance moved into the magnetic field.

steps.title: Calculate the induced emf
steps.content:
Now we substitute our values into the motional emf equation:
- $N = 120$
- $B = 0.40\text{ T}$
- $L = 0.10\text{ m}$
- $v = 2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= 120 \times 0.40 \times 0.10 \times 2.0 \\ \varepsilon &= 9.6\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the number of turns, field strength, length, and velocity.

calculator.description: Calculate the total induced emf using the motional formula.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The average induced emf during the time the coil enters the magnetic field is $9.6\text{ V}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct substitution of values and one mark for the final answer with correct units.

parts.content: Explain how the induced emf would change if the normal to the coil makes an angle $\theta$ (where $0 < \theta < 90^\circ$) with the magnetic field lines.
parts.markscheme:
If the normal makes an angle $\theta$, the effective flux linkage is reduced by a factor of $\cos\theta$ <marks>1</marks>
This would decrease the change in flux linkage and therefore the induced emf <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Relate emf to flux
steps.content:
According to Faraday's Law, the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage ($\Phi$). This concept is a core part of **Topic D.4: Induction**.

$$\varepsilon = N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Apply flux linkage formula
steps.content:
The magnetic flux linkage through a coil depends on the angle between the field and the coil's orientation. When the normal to the coil makes an angle $\theta$ with the magnetic field lines, the flux linkage is defined as:

$$\Phi = BA \cos\theta$$

highlights.text: normal to the coil makes an angle $\theta$ (where $0 < \theta < 90^\circ$) with the magnetic field lines

steps.title: Evaluate the angular factor
steps.content:
In the original scenario, the coil was perpendicular to the field ($\theta = 0^\circ$), so $\cos 0^\circ = 1$, giving maximum flux. 

For any angle in the range $0 < \theta < 90^\circ$, the value of $\cos\theta$ is always less than $1$. This effectively reduces the flux linkage by a factor of $\cos\theta$ compared to the perpendicular case.

gdc.instructions: Set the calculator to degrees. Enter cos(45).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press the [cos] key, type 45, and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set the graph to Degree mode. Type cos(45).

calculator.description: Verify that cos(theta) is less than 1 for an angle in the given range (e.g., 45 degrees).

steps.title: Determine the resulting emf
steps.content:
Because the coil is pulled at a constant velocity, the rate at which area enters the field is constant. However, since the flux linkage is now scaled by $\cos\theta$, the total change in flux linkage over the same time interval is smaller.

A smaller rate of change of flux linkage ($\frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$) results in a **smaller induced emf**.

steps.title: Identify the change in geometry
steps.content: Initially, the magnetic field was perpendicular to the plane of the coil, meaning the normal to the coil was parallel to the field lines ($ \theta = 0 $). Now, the normal is tilted at an angle $\theta$ relative to the magnetic field lines.

highlights.text: normal to the coil makes an angle θ

steps.title: Determine the effective field component
steps.content:
Using the **Effective Field Component Approach**, only the component of the magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the coil contributes to the magnetic flux. 

We resolve the magnetic field $B$ into this perpendicular component:
$$B_{\text{perpendicular}} = B \cos\theta$$

Since the question specifies $0 < \theta < 90^\circ$, we know that $\cos\theta < 1$, which means $B_{\text{perpendicular}} < B$.

steps.title: Relate to induced emf
steps.content:
According to Faraday's Law, the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is determined by the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage ($N\Phi$). Since magnetic flux is $\Phi = B_{\text{perpendicular}}A$, we can see that:

$$\varepsilon \propto B \cos\theta$$

Because the effective field component is smaller than the original field ($B \cos\theta < B$), the rate of change of flux linkage decreases. Therefore, the **induced emf is reduced** by a factor of $\cos\theta$.

highlights.text: effective flux linkage is reduced by a factor of cosθ

parts.content: State Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction.
parts.markscheme: The induced emf is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux through the coil: $\varepsilon = -N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the key concept
steps.content: This question asks for the definition of a fundamental principle in electromagnetism found in **Topic D.4 Induction**. We need to describe the relationship between a changing magnetic field and the voltage generated.

steps.title: State Faraday's law verbally
steps.content: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that the **induced electromotive force (emf)** is directly proportional to the **rate of change of magnetic flux linkage** (or magnetic flux).

steps.title: Express the relationship mathematically
steps.content:
In SI units, the constant of proportionality is 1. For a coil with $N$ turns, this is expressed as:

$$ \varepsilon = -N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} $$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ is the induced emf
- $N$ is the number of turns
- $\frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$ is the rate of change of magnetic flux

steps.title: Write the mathematical equation
steps.content:
To state Faraday's law using the mathematical statement approach, we write the formula that relates the induced electromotive force to the rate of change of magnetic flux:

$$ \varepsilon = -N \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} $$

steps.title: Define the symbols
steps.content:
To ensure your statement is complete, define each symbol used in the equation:

*   $\varepsilon$ is the **induced electromotive force (emf)**
*   $N$ is the **number of turns** in the coil
*   $\Delta \Phi$ is the **change in magnetic flux**
*   $\Delta t$ is the **time interval** taken for the change

(Note: The minus sign represents Lenz's Law, indicating the direction of the induced emf opposes the change producing it.)

parts.content: Calculate the magnitude of the average induced emf in the coil.
parts.markscheme:
- $\varepsilon = 150 \cdot \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.10} = 150 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.30\ \text{V}$
- Correct <marks> 1 mark </marks> substitution  and value <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, identify the values provided in the question for the number of turns, the magnetic flux, and the time interval:

*   Number of turns: $N = 150$
*   Initial flux: $\Phi_{\text{initial}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Wb}$
*   Final flux: $\Phi_{\text{final}} = 0\ \text{Wb}$
*   Time interval: $\Delta t = 0.10\ \text{s}$

steps.title: State Faraday's Law
steps.content:
To find the magnitude of the average induced emf, we use Faraday's Law. The formula relates the induced emf $\varepsilon$ to the number of turns $N$ and the rate of change of magnetic flux $\frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$:

$$\varepsilon = N \frac{|\Delta \Phi|}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Calculate change in flux
steps.content:
Determine the magnitude of the change in magnetic flux $|\Delta \Phi|$.

$$\begin{aligned} |\Delta \Phi| &= |\Phi_{\text{final}} - \Phi_{\text{initial}}| \\ &= |0 - 2.0 \times 10^{-4}| \\ &= 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Wb} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the change in flux

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula to calculate the induced emf.

$$\varepsilon = 150 \times \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.10}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final emf value

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Solving the expression gives the magnitude of the average induced emf.

$$\varepsilon = 0.30\ \text{V}$$

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the parameters provided in the question specification:

*   Number of turns: $N = 150$
*   Initial magnetic flux: $\Phi_{\text{initial}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Wb}$
*   Final magnetic flux: $\Phi_{\text{final}} = 0\ \text{Wb}$
*   Time interval: $\Delta t = 0.10\ \text{s}$

steps.title: Calculate change in flux
steps.content:
We calculate the change in magnetic flux ($\Delta \Phi$) by subtracting the initial flux from the final flux:

$$\Delta \Phi = \Phi_{\text{final}} - \Phi_{\text{initial}}$$

$$\Delta \Phi = 0 - 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = -2.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Wb}$$

calculator.description: Calculate change in flux

steps.title: Calculate change in flux linkage
steps.content:
Using the Flux Linkage Approach, we find the total change in flux linkage ($\Delta(N\Phi)$) by multiplying the change in flux by the number of turns $N$:

$$\Delta(N\Phi) = N \cdot \Delta \Phi$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta(N\Phi) = 150 \cdot (-2.0 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.030\ \text{Wb turns}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply N by change in flux

ti84.instructions: Multiply N by change in flux

steps.title: Calculate magnitude of induced emf
steps.content:
According to Faraday's Law, the magnitude of the average induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is the rate of change of the flux linkage magnitude. We divide the magnitude of the flux linkage change by the time interval:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{|\Delta(N\Phi)|}{\Delta t}$$

Substituting our calculated values:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{|-0.030|}{0.10} = \frac{0.030}{0.10}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the final division gives the average induced emf:

$$\varepsilon = 0.30\ \text{V}$$

The magnitude of the average induced emf is **0.30 V**.

parts.content: Explain how Lenz’s law determines the direction of the induced emf.
parts.markscheme: Lenz’s law states that the induced emf produces a current whose magnetic field opposes the change in flux that caused it <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: State the fundamental principle
steps.content:
Lenz's law determines the direction of the induced emf based on the principle of opposition. 

The law states that the **direction of the induced emf** (and any resulting current) is always such that it acts to **oppose the change in magnetic flux** that created it.

steps.title: Explain the mechanism of opposition
steps.content:
To oppose the change, the induced emf drives an induced current. This current generates its own magnetic field.

*   If the magnetic flux is **decreasing** (as in this question, where it goes to zero), the induced magnetic field will point in the **same direction** as the original field to try and maintain the flux.
*   If the magnetic flux were **increasing**, the induced magnetic field would point in the **opposite direction** to resist the increase.

steps.title: Summary
steps.content:
Therefore, the direction is determined by finding which way the current must flow to create a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux.

This concept is part of **Topic D.4 Induction**.

parts.content: State the condition under which a moving conductor in a magnetic field induces an emf.
parts.markscheme:

steps.title: Understand the Cutting Concept
steps.content: To induce an electromotive force (emf) in a moving conductor, we use the **Magnetic Flux Cutting Approach**. Imagine the magnetic field lines as physical lines in space. For an emf to be generated, the conductor must physically cross or "cut" through these lines as it moves. This process is a fundamental part of electromagnetic induction, which you can study further in **Topic D: Fields**.

steps.title: Identify the Motion Requirement
steps.content: If a conductor moves perfectly parallel to the magnetic field lines, it simply slides along them without crossing any. In this case, no magnetic flux is "cut," and therefore, no emf is induced. For induction to occur, there must be a component of velocity that is perpendicular to the field.

steps.title: State the Final Condition
steps.content:
The specific condition is that the conductor must move in a direction that is not parallel to the magnetic field lines. By moving at an angle, the conductor effectively cuts across the field lines, which induces the emf. 

According to the markscheme, you should state that:
- The conductor moves at an angle to the magnetic field lines (or simply that it "cuts" the magnetic field lines).

highlights.text: The conductor moves at an angle to the magnetic field lines (cuts magnetic field lines)

steps.title: Identify the core principle
steps.content:
To understand when an electromotive force (emf) is induced, we look at **Faraday’s Law of Induction**. This principle is covered in **Topic D.4 (Induction)**. 

Faraday's Law states that an emf is induced in a conductor whenever there is a **change in magnetic flux linkage** over time. For a moving conductor, this change occurs when the conductor moves in a way that it "sweeps through" or "cuts" the magnetic field lines.

steps.title: Analyze the motion
steps.content:
Imagine the conductor moving through space. If it moves perfectly parallel to the magnetic field lines, it doesn't cross any of them. In this case, the magnetic flux linkage remains zero (or constant), and no emf is induced.

However, if the conductor moves at an angle (like the $90^{\circ}$ angle mentioned in the question), it will "cut" across the field lines. This action creates a rate of change of flux linkage as the conductor sweeps across the magnetic field.

steps.title: State the condition
steps.content:
Based on the Faraday’s Law approach, the necessary condition is that the motion of the conductor must not be parallel to the magnetic field lines.

**Final Answer:** The conductor must move at an angle to the magnetic field lines (it must **cut** the magnetic field lines).

steps.title: Consider the microscopic view
steps.content: To understand how an emf is induced, we look at the free electrons inside the conductor. When the conductor moves, these electrons move along with it at the same velocity. If this movement happens within a magnetic field, the electrons may experience a magnetic force known as the Lorentz force.

steps.title: Apply the Lorentz force
steps.content:
According to the **Fields** section of your data booklet, the force $F$ on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $v$ in a magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

For an emf to be induced, this force must push the electrons toward one end of the conductor, creating a separation of charge (a potential difference).

steps.title: Identify the critical condition
steps.content:
From the formula $$F = qvB\sin\theta$$, we can see that if the conductor moves parallel to the magnetic field lines, the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$. 

Since $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$, the magnetic force on the electrons would be zero, and no emf would be induced. Therefore, for an induction to occur, the conductor must move at an angle to the field lines so that it "cuts" across them.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The condition for inducing an emf is that the conductor must move at an angle to the magnetic field lines, rather than moving parallel to them. This is often described as the conductor "cutting" the magnetic field lines.

Using the Faraday's Law approach, the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkage ($\Phi$). 

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t}$$

As the conductor moves, it "sweeps out" a rectangular area in the magnetic field. In a small time interval $\Delta t$, the distance moved is $v\Delta t$. The change in area ($\Delta A$) is:

$$\Delta A = L \times (v\Delta t)$$

The change in magnetic flux is the field strength multiplied by this area:

$$\Delta\Phi = B \times \Delta A = B L v \Delta t$$

steps.title: Calculate the induced emf
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the flux change back into Faraday's Law. Notice that the $\Delta t$ terms cancel out, leaving us with the standard formula for motional emf:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{B L v \Delta t}{\Delta t} = BvL$$

Substituting our values into the formula:

$$\varepsilon = 0.40 \times 6.0 \times 0.50$$

steps.title: The Flux Cutting Concept
steps.content: According to the **flux cutting concept**, an electromotive force (emf) is only induced when a conductor moves in a direction that causes it to "cut" across magnetic field lines. This interaction between the moving charges in the conductor and the magnetic field is what generates the potential difference.

steps.title: Analyze Parallel Motion
steps.content: When the conductor moves **parallel to the magnetic field**, its velocity vector is aligned with the magnetic field lines. This means the conductor is sliding along the lines rather than crossing over them. Because the conductor does not cross or intersect any field lines, the number of field lines cut per second is zero.

steps.title: State the Result
steps.content:
Since no magnetic field lines are being cut, there is no change in the magnetic environment relative to the conductor's charges. Therefore, **no emf is induced** ($0\text{ V}$).

You can also see this in the formula for the magnetic force on a charge:

$$\text{Force} = qvB\sin\theta$$

When the motion is parallel, the angle $\theta$ between the velocity $v$ and the magnetic field $B$ is $0^\circ$. Since $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$, the force (and the resulting emf) is zero.

steps.title: Identify the motional emf formula
steps.content:
To determine the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) in a conductor moving through a magnetic field, we use the motional emf formula:

$$\varepsilon = BvL \sin\theta$$

In this equation:
- $B$ is the magnetic field strength ($0.40\text{ T}$)
- $v$ is the velocity ($6.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$)
- $L$ is the length of the conductor ($0.50\text{ m}$)
- $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field lines.

steps.title: Determine the angle
steps.content:
The question asks what happens when the conductor is moved **parallel** to the magnetic field. 

When two vectors are parallel, the angle between them is $\theta = 0^\circ$.

steps.title: Calculate the induced emf
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $\theta = 0^\circ$ into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= BvL \sin(0^\circ) \\ \varepsilon &= 0.40 \cdot 6.0 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 0 \\ \varepsilon &= 0\text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

Since $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$, the entire product becomes zero, meaning no emf is induced.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Because the conductor moves parallel to the field, it does not "cut" any magnetic field lines. Therefore, there is no change in flux linkage, and the induced emf is zero.

This concept is a key part of **Topic D.4: Induction**. If you're unsure why cutting lines matters, reviewing Faraday's Law in that chapter will help!

steps.title: Identify the Lorentz force
steps.content:
To understand induced emf from a microscopic level, we look at the magnetic force acting on the free charge carriers (electrons) within the conductor. This is known as the Lorentz force. From the data booklet, the force $F$ on a charge $q$ is:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

Where:
- $v$ is the velocity of the conductor.
- $B$ is the magnetic field strength.
- $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field.

steps.title: Evaluate the angle
steps.content:
When the conductor moves **parallel** to the magnetic field, the angle $\theta$ between the velocity vector and the magnetic field lines is $0^{\circ}$ (or $180^{\circ}$ if moving in the opposite direction). 

Let's check the value of $\sin(0^{\circ})$ using a calculator.

steps.title: Determine the resulting force
steps.content:
Substituting $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ into the Lorentz force equation:

$$\begin{aligned} F &= qvB \sin(0^{\circ}) \\ F &= qvB(0) \\ F &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since there is no magnetic force acting on the charges, they do not migrate to the ends of the conductor.

steps.title: Conclude the induced emf
steps.content:
Because there is no force to cause charge separation, no potential difference is established across the conductor. 

Therefore, **no emf is induced** when the motion is parallel to the magnetic field.

steps.title: Identify the relevant concept
steps.content:
In this problem, we have a conducting bar moving through a uniform magnetic field. When a straight conductor moves perpendicularly through a magnetic field, the free charges within it experience a magnetic force, leading to an induced electromotive force (emf) across its ends. 

This concept is part of **Topic D.4 (Induction)**. The specific formula we use for a straight conductor is:
$$\varepsilon = BvL$$
Where:
- $\varepsilon$ is the induced emf
- $B$ is the magnetic field strength
- $v$ is the velocity of the bar
- $L$ is the length of the bar

steps.title: Calculate the induced emf
steps.content:

steps.title: Apply Faraday's Law
steps.content:
To find the induced electromotive force (emf), we use **Faraday's Law of Induction**. This principle states that the magnitude of the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is equal to the rate of change of magnetic flux ($\Phi$) through the circuit.

This concept is covered in **Topic D.4: Induction**. According to Faraday's Law:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Calculate swept area
steps.content:
As the bar moves, it "sweeps out" a rectangular area through the magnetic field. Magnetic flux is defined as $\Phi = BA$. Therefore, the change in flux is caused by the change in the area covered by the circuit:

$$\Delta \Phi = B \times \Delta A$$

In a small time interval $\Delta t$, the bar moves a distance $\Delta s = v\Delta t$. The area swept out is the length of the bar ($L$) multiplied by this distance:

$$\Delta A = L \times (v\Delta t)$$

Substituting this back into Faraday's Law gives us the motional emf formula used in the markscheme:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{B \times L \times v \times \Delta t}{\Delta t} = BLv$$

- Velocity $v = 3.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
- Length $L = 0.60\ \text{m}$

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= 0.50 \times 3.0 \times 0.60 \\ \varepsilon &= 0.90\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate induced EMF
steps.content:
As the bar moves through the magnetic field, a **motional EMF** ($\varepsilon$) is induced across its ends. In IB Physics **Topic D.4 (Induction)**, we learn that when a conductor moves perpendicularly through a uniform magnetic field, the induced EMF is calculated as:

$$\varepsilon = BvL$$

Substituting our values:

Now that we have the induced EMF, we can find the current ($I$) using Ohm's Law from **Topic B.5 (Current and circuits)**. Since the EMF acts like the voltage source for the circuit, we use the formula:

steps.title: Relate flux to motion
steps.content:
According to Faraday’s Law, an electromotive force (emf) is induced when there is a change in magnetic flux linkage. As the bar moves, it sweeps out an area, which changes the magnetic flux $\Phi$ passing through the circuit loop.

The magnetic flux is given by:
$$\Phi = BA$$

Since the magnetic field $B$ is constant, the rate of change of flux (the induced emf $\varepsilon$) is proportional to the rate at which the area $A$ changes:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t} = B \frac{\Delta A}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Calculate the induced emf
steps.content:
The area swept out by the bar of length $L$ moving a distance $\Delta x$ in time $\Delta t$ is $\Delta A = L \Delta x$. Since velocity $v = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$, the rate of change of area is $\frac{\Delta A}{\Delta t} = Lv$.

Substituting the given values into the formula for induced emf:
$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= B L v \\ \varepsilon &= 0.50 \times 0.60 \times 3.0 \\ \varepsilon &= 0.90 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation is based on concepts from **Topic D.4: Induction**.

calculator.description: Calculate the induced emf (B * L * v).

steps.title: Determine the current
steps.content:
Now we use Ohm's Law to find the current $I$ in the circuit. Ohm's Law states that current is the ratio of potential difference (or emf) to resistance:

$$I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R}$$

Using the calculated emf of $0.90 \text{ V}$ and the given resistance of $2.0 \, \Omega$:
$$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{0.90}{2.0} \\ I &= 0.45 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide the emf by the resistance to find the current.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The current in the circuit is $0.45\ \text{A}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn the mark by correctly substituting the emf value into the current formula to reach the final result.

steps.title: Use Power Balance
steps.content:
To find the current, we can use the principle of energy conservation. The mechanical power ($P_{\text{mech}}$) used to move the bar through the magnetic field must equal the electrical power ($P_{\text{elec}}$) dissipated by the resistor. This is a great alternative to the standard EMF method!

This approach draws on concepts from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power** and **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

steps.title: Determine Mechanical Power
steps.content:
Mechanical power is defined as the product of force and velocity ($P = Fv$). The force resisting the motion is the magnetic force on the current-carrying bar. 

From the data booklet, the magnetic force is:
$$F = BIL\sin\theta$$

Since the bar is moving perpendicular to the field, $\sin\theta = 1$. Thus, the mechanical power input is:
$$P_{\text{mech}} = (BIL) \times v$$

Substituting our known values:
$$P_{\text{mech}} = (0.50 \times I \times 0.60) \times 3.0$$

steps.title: Determine Electrical Power
steps.content:
Electrical power dissipated in a resistor is given by the formula:
$$P_{\text{elec}} = I^2 R$$

Given the total resistance of the circuit is $2.0\ \Omega$, we have:
$$P_{\text{elec}} = I^2 \times 2.0$$

highlights.text: total resistance of the circuit is 2.0 \Omega

steps.title: Solve for Current
steps.content:
Now we equate the two power expressions and solve for $I$:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{elec}} &= P_{\text{mech}} \\ I^2 \times 2.0 &= (0.50 \times I \times 0.60) \times 3.0 \\ 2.0 I^2 &= 0.90 I \end{aligned}$$

Dividing both sides by $I$ (since $I \neq 0$):
$$\begin{aligned} 2.0 I &= 0.90 \\ I &= \frac{0.90}{2.0} \\ I &= 0.45\ \text{A} \end{aligned}$$

To find the power using mechanical work, we first need the induced current $I$. The motional EMF $\varepsilon$ generated by the moving bar is given by $\varepsilon = BvL$.

steps.title: Identify the initial energy
steps.content:
To understand the energy flow, we first look at the state of the bar. The question states the bar is moving at a constant velocity. This movement represents **mechanical energy** (specifically kinetic energy).

If you want to review the basics of kinetic energy, check out **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

highlights.text: moves to the right at 3.0 m s−1

steps.title: Mechanical to electrical conversion
steps.content:
As the bar moves through the uniform magnetic field, it cuts through magnetic flux lines. According to the principles of electromagnetic induction, this motion induces an electromotive force (emf) and a current in the circuit. 

In this step, the **mechanical energy** required to move the bar is converted into **electrical energy**.

steps.title: Dissipation as thermal energy
steps.content:
Once the electrical energy is generated, the resulting current flows through the resistor. Because the circuit has a total resistance of $2.0\ \Omega$, this electrical energy doesn't stay in the circuit forever. 

Instead, it is dissipated as **thermal energy** (often called Joule heating) as it passes through the resistor. This is the final stage of our energy sequence.

highlights.text: total resistance of the circuit is 2.0 Ω

steps.title: Summarize the energy flow
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, you must clearly state the sequence:

1. The **mechanical energy** of the moving bar is converted into **electrical energy** via induction.
2. This **electrical energy** is then dissipated as **thermal energy** (heat) in the resistor.

steps.title: Analyze Lenz's Law
steps.content:
To understand energy transformations using the **Work and Power Analysis** approach, we first look at why work is required. 

According to **Lenz's Law**, the induced current creates a magnetic force ($F = BIL$) that opposes the motion of the bar. To keep the bar moving at a constant speed, an external force must do work against this magnetic opposition. This mechanical work done per unit time is the mechanical power input.

steps.title: Mechanical to Electrical Energy
steps.content:
The work done by the external agent to move the bar through the magnetic field is not lost; it is transformed into **electrical energy**. 

The movement of the bar through the field induces an electromotive force (emf), which drives a current through the circuit. This is the first step of the transformation: 

$$\text{Mechanical Work (Energy)} \rightarrow \text{Electrical Energy}$$

highlights.text: The mechanical energy of the moving bar is converted into electrical energy

steps.title: Electrical to Thermal Energy
steps.content:
Once the electrical energy is generated, it flows through the resistor. As the electrons collide with the lattice ions in the resistor, the electrical energy is dissipated as **internal energy** (commonly referred to as thermal energy or heat). 

This process is known as Joule heating. The final transformation step is:

$$\text{Electrical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Thermal Energy}$$

highlights.text: This electrical energy is dissipated as thermal energy (Joule heating) in the resistor

steps.title: Verify Power Conservation
steps.content:
We can verify this analysis by calculating the power. In a Work and Power Analysis, the mechanical power input ($P = Fv$) must equal the electrical power dissipated ($P = I^2 R$). 

1. First, find the induced emf: $\epsilon = Bvl = 0.50 \times 3.0 \times 0.60 = 0.90\text{ V}$
2. Find the current: $I = \frac{\epsilon}{R} = \frac{0.90}{2.0} = 0.45\text{ A}$
3. Calculate Electrical Power: $P_{elec} = I^2 R = (0.45)^2 \times 2.0 = 0.405\text{ W}$
4. Calculate Mechanical Power: $F_{mag} = BIL = 0.50 \times 0.45 \times 0.60 = 0.135\text{ N}$, so $P_{mech} = Fv = 0.135 \times 3.0 = 0.405\text{ W}$

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
The energy transformation sequence is:

1. **Mechanical energy** (work done to move the bar) is converted into **electrical energy** due to electromagnetic induction.
2. **Electrical energy** is then converted into **thermal energy** as it is dissipated by the resistor.

steps.title: Identify given information

To calculate the magnetic flux, we first extract the relevant values from the problem statement:

- Magnetic field strength ($B$): $0.30\ \text{T}$
- Area of the coil ($A$): $0.020\ \text{m}^2$

While the coil has $N = 200$ turns and a frequency $f = 60\ \text{Hz}$, these are not required to find the magnetic flux through a single turn.

steps.title: Apply magnetic flux definition
steps.content:
The magnetic flux $\Phi$ through a surface is defined by the equation:

$$\Phi = BA \cos \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the normal to the plane of the coil. 

The **maximum** magnetic flux occurs when the magnetic field lines are perpendicular to the plane of the coil (parallel to the normal). In this case, $\theta = 0^\circ$ and $\cos(0^\circ) = 1$. 

Therefore, the maximum flux formula simplifies to:

$$\Phi_{\text{max}} = BA$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum flux
steps.content:
Substitute the given values into the simplified equation:

$$\Phi_{\text{max}} = 0.30\ \text{T} \times 0.020\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\Phi_{\text{max}} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$$

This calculation provides the maximum flux through the area of the coil. In the IB markscheme, 1 mark is awarded for this correct numerical value and unit.

parts.content: Explain why the induced emf is sinusoidal.
parts.markscheme:
The magnetic flux varies as $\cos(\omega t)$ (or $\sin(\omega t)$) as the coil rotates <marks>A1</marks>

By Faraday's law, the induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of flux (derivative), which is sinusoidal <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the rotating coil
steps.content:
To understand why the induced emf is sinusoidal, we first need to look at how the magnetic flux linkage changes as the coil rotates. 

In this setup, a coil is rotating at a constant frequency $f = 60\ \text{Hz}$ in a uniform magnetic field. As it turns, the angle $\theta$ between the magnetic field lines and the normal to the coil's area changes continuously.

highlights.text: Explain why the induced emf is sinusoidal.

steps.title: Flux as a cosine function
steps.content:
The magnetic flux linkage through the $N$ turns of the coil is given by the expression:

$$\Phi = NBA \cos(\theta)$$

Since the coil rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega$, the angle at any time $t$ can be written as $\theta = \omega t$. Substituting this in, we get:

$$\Phi(t) = NBA \cos(\omega t)$$

This shows that the magnetic flux linkage varies as a cosine function over time.

highlights.text: The magnetic flux varies as \cos(\omega t) (or \sin(\omega t)) as the coil rotates

steps.title: Apply Faraday's Law
steps.content:
According to **Faraday’s Law**, the magnitude of the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is equal to the rate of change of the magnetic flux linkage:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{\Delta \Phi}{\Delta t}$$

In calculus terms, the emf is the negative derivative of the flux linkage with respect to time ($-\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$). This concept is covered in **Topic D.4: Induction**.

steps.title: Conclusion: Sinusoidal emf
steps.content:
Mathematically, the derivative of a cosine function is a sine function (specifically, $\frac{d}{dt}\cos(\omega t) = -\omega \sin(\omega t)$). 

Therefore, the induced emf is given by:

$$\varepsilon(t) = NBA\omega \sin(\omega t)$$

Because the rate of change of a sinusoidal function is itself sinusoidal, the resulting induced emf follows a sine wave pattern.

highlights.text: By Faraday's law, the induced emf is proportional to the rate of change of flux (derivative), which is sinusoidal

steps.title: Identify the cutting mechanism
steps.content:
As the coil rotates, its sides move through the uniform magnetic field. We can think of the induced emf as being generated because these sides "cut" across the magnetic field lines. 

This is the concept of **motional emf**, where a conductor moving through a magnetic field experiences a separation of charges due to the magnetic force.

steps.title: Analyze velocity components
steps.content:
For a conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ in a magnetic field $B$, the induced emf $\varepsilon$ depends only on the component of the velocity that is perpendicular to the field lines ($v_{\perp}$).

If the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field lines is $\theta$, then:

$$v_{\perp} = v \sin(\theta)$$ 

This relationship is derived from the magnetic force equation found in your data booklet:

$$\text{Force on a moving charge: } F = qvB\sin\theta$$

steps.title: Relate angle to rotation
steps.content:
As the coil rotates at a constant frequency $f$, the angle $\theta$ changes at a constant rate. We define this using the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\theta = \omega t$$

Substituting this into our velocity component expression, we see that the perpendicular velocity (and thus the rate of cutting flux) varies over time:

$$v_{\perp} \propto \sin(\omega t)$$

steps.title: Conclude sinusoidal nature
steps.content:
Since the induced emf $\varepsilon$ is proportional to the rate at which the wire cuts the flux lines, and that rate varies with $\sin(\omega t)$, the resulting emf is:

$$\varepsilon \propto \sin(\omega t)$$

Any quantity that varies according to a sine or cosine function over time is described as **sinusoidal**. This explains why the output of the generator is an alternating current (AC) signal.

steps.title: Calculate the coil area
steps.content:
To find the magnetic flux, we first need the cross-sectional area $A$ of the circular coil. Since the radius is given as $0.10\ \text{m}$, we use the formula for the area of a circle:

$$A = \pi r^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$A = \pi \times (0.10)^2 = 0.01\pi \approx 0.0314\ \text{m}^2$$

steps.title: Express magnetic flux linkage
steps.content:
Magnetic flux linkage $\Phi$ is the product of the number of turns $N$, the magnetic field $B(t)$, and the area $A$. We are given $N = 200$ and $B(t) = 0.30 \sin(120\pi t)$.

$$\Phi(t) = N \cdot B(t) \cdot A$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \Phi(t) &= 200 \times [0.30 \sin(120\pi t)] \times 0.01\pi \\ \Phi(t) &= 0.6\pi \sin(120\pi t) \\ \Phi(t) &\approx 1.885 \sin(120\pi t) \end{aligned}$$

This represents the total flux linking the 200 turns of the coil at any time $t$.

steps.title: Apply Faraday's Law
steps.content:
Faraday's Law states that the induced electromotive force (emf) is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage. Using calculus, this is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon(t) = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

We need to differentiate our expression for $\Phi(t)$ with respect to time $t$. Recall that $\frac{d}{dt}[\sin(kt)] = k \cos(kt)$.

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon(t) &= -\frac{d}{dt} [0.6\pi \sin(120\pi t)] \\ \varepsilon(t) &= -0.6\pi \times (120\pi) \cos(120\pi t) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final expression
steps.content:
Now we simplify the numerical coefficients. 

$$\varepsilon(t) = -(0.6 \times 120 \times \pi^2) \cos(120\pi t)$$
$$\varepsilon(t) = -72\pi^2 \cos(120\pi t)$$

Calculating the value:
$$72 \times \pi^2 \approx 710.6$$

Rounding to an appropriate number of significant figures, we get:
$$\varepsilon(t) \approx -710 \cos(120\pi t)\ \text{V}$$

steps.title: Determine peak induced EMF
steps.content:
According to the principles of induction for a sinusoidally varying field, the maximum (peak) induced EMF ($\varepsilon_0$) is given by the product of the number of turns, the peak magnetic field, the area, and the angular frequency:

$$\varepsilon_0 = N B_0 A \omega$$

Substituting our values:
$$\varepsilon_0 = 200 \times 0.30 \times (0.01\pi) \times 120\pi$$
$$\varepsilon_0 = 72\pi^2 \approx 710.6\ \text{V}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the peak EMF magnitude.

steps.title: Apply phase and time-dependence
steps.content:
Using Faraday's Law, $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$. Since $B(t)$ varies as $\sin(\omega t)$, the derivative results in a $\cos(\omega t)$ term with a negative sign (due to Lenz's Law):

$$\frac{d}{dt}[\sin(\omega t)] = \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Applying this to our peak value, the induced emf as a function of time is:
$$\varepsilon(t) = -\varepsilon_0 \cos(120\pi t)$$
$$\varepsilon(t) \approx -710 \cos(120\pi t)\ \text{V}$$

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The final expression for the induced EMF includes the calculated magnitude and the correct trigonometric phase shift:

$$\varepsilon(t) = -710 \cos(120\pi t)\ \text{V}$$

**Marking Note:** One mark is awarded for the correct numerical coefficient and expression, and one mark for including the correct units (Volts).

parts.content: Explain how this setup demonstrates Lenz’s law and conservation of energy.
parts.markscheme:
- The induced emf produces a current whose magnetic field opposes the change in external flux (Lenz’s law) <marks>R1</marks>
- This ensures that energy is not created or destroyed; the kinetic energy of the oscillating magnet is converted into electrical energy in the coil <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Define Lenz's Law
steps.content:
As the magnet oscillates, the magnetic field $B(t) = 0.30 \sin(120\pi t)$ through the coil changes, which induces an electromotive force (emf) and a current. According to **Lenz’s law**, the direction of this induced current is such that the magnetic field it creates **opposes the change** in the external magnetic flux that produced it.

This means if the magnet is moving towards the coil, the coil creates a magnetic field to repel it; if it moves away, the coil creates a field to attract it.

highlights.text: The induced emf produces a current whose magnetic field opposes the change in external flux (Lenz’s law)

steps.title: Identify Energy Transformation
steps.content:
This opposition is a requirement of the **Law of Conservation of Energy**. Because the coil exerts a force that opposes the magnet's motion, work must be done for the magnet to keep moving. 

In this system, the **kinetic energy** of the oscillating magnet is continuously **converted into electrical energy** in the coil. If Lenz’s law were not true (if the field aided the change), energy would be created from nothing, violating the conservation of energy.

highlights.text: the kinetic energy of the oscillating magnet is converted into electrical energy in the coil

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To secure full marks on this question, ensure you clearly state:
1.  **Lenz's Law:** The induced current creates a field that opposes the flux change (1 mark).
2.  **Conservation of Energy:** Mechanical/Kinetic energy of the magnet is transformed into electrical energy (1 mark).

steps.title: Link Lenz's Law to Force
steps.content: As the magnet oscillates, the magnetic flux through the coil changes, inducing an emf and a current. According to **Lenz's law**, the induced current creates a magnetic field that opposes the change in flux. From a **work-energy perspective**, this means the coil exerts a **resistive magnetic force** on the magnet that always opposes its motion (e.g., pushing back as the magnet approaches and pulling as it retreats).

highlights.text: The induced emf produces a current whose magnetic field opposes the change in external flux (Lenz’s law)

steps.title: Apply Conservation of Energy
steps.content:
To maintain the magnet's oscillation, work must be done to overcome this resistive force. This demonstrates the **law of conservation of energy**: the mechanical energy (kinetic energy) lost by the magnet is not destroyed, but is instead transformed into the electrical energy flowing through the coil.

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

If the induced field did not oppose the motion, energy would be created from nothing, which is impossible.

highlights.text: This ensures that energy is not created or destroyed; the kinetic energy of the oscillating magnet is converted into electrical energy in the coil

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In summary:
1. **Lenz’s Law**: The induced current's magnetic field creates a resistive force against the magnet.
2. **Conservation of Energy**: Work done against this resistance converts the magnet's kinetic energy into electrical energy in the coil.

This concept is a key part of **Topic D.4 (Induction)** and **Topic A.3 (Work, Energy and Power)**.

steps.title: Identify required variables
steps.content:
To calculate the maximum magnetic flux through the coil, we need the magnetic field strength ($B$) and the area of a single turn ($A$). 

From the question, we have:
- Magnetic field strength: $B = 0.40\ \text{T}$
- Area of one turn: $A = 0.015\ \text{m}^2$

Note: While the question mentions the number of turns ($N = 250$), magnetic flux ($̄\Phi$) typically refers to the flux through a single turn. The total flux through all turns is called flux linkage ($N\Phi$).

steps.title: Apply magnetic flux formula
steps.content:
The magnetic flux $\Phi$ is defined as the product of the magnetic field strength and the area perpendicular to that field. 

Maximum flux occurs when the coil is perpendicular to the magnetic field. We use the formula:

$$\Phi_{\max} = BA$$

This concept is part of **Topic D.4: Induction**. Although the formula for flux is often written as $\Phi = BA\cos\theta$, the maximum value is simply $BA$ (when $\cos\theta = 1$).

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Substitute the given values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \Phi_{\max} &= 0.40 \times 0.015 \\ \Phi_{\max} &= 0.0060\ \text{Wb} \end{aligned}$$

In scientific notation, this is expressed as $6.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb}$. We provide the answer to two significant figures to match the precision of the given data.

The magnetic flux linkage $\Phi$ through the $N$ turns of the coil is given by:

This concept is a core part of **Topic D.4: Induction**.

steps.title: Apply Faraday's Law
steps.content:
Faraday's Law states that the induced emf ($\varepsilon$) is equal to the negative rate of change of magnetic flux linkage:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

By differentiating our expression for $\Phi$ with respect to time $t$:

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon &= -\frac{d}{dt} (NBA \cos(\omega t)) \\ &= -NBA (-\omega \sin(\omega t)) \\ &= NBA\omega \sin(\omega t) \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
The induced emf is a sine wave. The maximum or "peak" value ($\varepsilon_{\max}$) occurs when the value of the $\sin(\omega t)$ term is $1$:

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = NAB\omega$$

parts.content: Explain the effect on the induced emf if the number of turns is doubled and the angular frequency is halved.
parts.markscheme:
- Doubling $N$ doubles emf; halving $\omega$ halves emf, so net emf remains the same <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- The frequency of emf oscillations decreases because angular frequency is halved <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the peak EMF formula
steps.content:
To understand how the induced emf changes, we start with the formula for the maximum (peak) induced emf ($\varepsilon_0$) in a rotating coil. 

This formula is derived from Faraday's Law and is standard for AC generators:
$$\varepsilon_0 = NBA\omega$$

Where:
- $N$ is the number of turns
- $B$ is the magnetic field strength
- $A$ is the area of the coil
- $\omega$ is the angular frequency

steps.title: Analyze changes to peak EMF
steps.content:
The question specifies two changes:
1. The number of turns is doubled ($N \rightarrow 2N$).
2. The angular frequency is halved ($\omega \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\omega$).

Let's substitute these into our formula for the new peak emf ($\varepsilon'_0$):
$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon'_0 &= (2N) \cdot B \cdot A \cdot (\frac{1}{2}\omega) \\ \varepsilon'_0 &= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (NBA\omega) \\ \varepsilon'_0 &= NBA\omega \end{aligned}$$

Because the factor of $2$ and the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ cancel out, the **maximum induced emf remains the same**.

steps.title: Determine the oscillation frequency
steps.content:
Next, we consider the frequency of the oscillations. The frequency ($f$) of the induced alternating emf is directly proportional to the angular frequency ($\omega$):

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

Since the angular frequency $\omega$ is halved, the **oscillation frequency of the emf will also be halved** (it decreases). This means the peaks of the emf will occur less often over time.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks, your explanation should clearly state two points:
1. The **net peak emf remains unchanged** because the doubling of $N$ is exactly compensated by the halving of $\omega$.
2. The **frequency of the emf oscillations decreases** (it is halved) because it depends directly on the rotation speed $\omega$.

steps.title: Recall Faraday's Law
steps.content:
To understand how the induced emf changes, we look at Faraday’s law. It states that the magnitude of the induced emf is proportional to the **rate of change of magnetic flux linkage**.

Flux linkage is the product of the number of turns ($N$) and the magnetic flux through a single loop ($\Phi$). Therefore, the induced emf depends on both how many turns we have and how quickly the magnetic field through them is changing.

steps.title: Effect of Doubling Turns
steps.content:
The question states that the **number of turns is doubled**. 

$$\text{New } N = 2 \times 250 = 500$$

Since emf is directly proportional to the number of turns, doubling $N$ doubles the total flux linkage. If all other factors remained the same, this change alone would double the magnitude of the induced emf.

steps.title: Effect of Halving Frequency
steps.content:
Next, we consider that the **angular frequency is halved**. 

$$\text{New } \omega = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

Angular frequency ($\omega$) determines how fast the coil rotates. If the coil rotates half as fast, the magnetic flux through the loops changes half as quickly. According to Faraday's Law, halving the rate of change of flux results in halving the induced emf.

steps.title: Determine Net Peak EMF
steps.content:
Now we combine these two effects:
1. Doubling the turns increases the emf by a factor of 2.
2. Halving the angular frequency decreases the emf by a factor of 0.5.

$$\text{Net Change} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$$

Because these two effects cancel each other out exactly, the **magnitude of the peak induced emf remains the same** (600 V).

steps.content:
Finally, we must consider the timing of the signal. The frequency of the electrical output is directly proportional to the rotation speed of the coil. 

Since the angular frequency $\omega$ is halved, the **frequency of the emf oscillations is also halved**. This means it will take twice as long for the emf to complete one full cycle (the period doubles).

steps.title: Select the flux formula

steps.title: State Power Conservation Principle
steps.content:
This problem involves converting mechanical energy into electrical energy. According to the principle of conservation of energy, the rate of mechanical work done to move the rod (Mechanical Power) must equal the rate at which energy is dissipated as heat in the resistor (Electrical Power).

$$P_{\text{mech}} = P_{\text{elec}}$$ 

This approach links the motion of the rod directly to the current without explicitly calculating the induced EMF first.

steps.title: Define Mechanical Power
steps.content:
Mechanical power is the product of force and velocity ($P = Fv$). The force acting on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by the formula in the **Fields** section of the data booklet. Since the motion is perpendicular to the field ($	heta = 90^\circ$, $\sin 90^\circ = 1$), the magnetic force is:

$$F = BIL$$

Substituting this into the power equation gives the mechanical power input:

$$P_{\text{mech}} = (BIL) \times v = BILv$$

steps.title: Define Electrical Power
steps.content:
The electrical power dissipated by a resistor is given by the fundamental definition:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = I^2R$$

steps.title: Equate and solve for Current
steps.content:
Now we equate the mechanical power input to the electrical power dissipated and solve for the current $I(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{mech}} &= P_{\text{elec}} \\ BILv &= I^2R \end{aligned}$$

Dividing both sides by $I$ (where $I \neq 0$):

$$\begin{aligned} BLv &= IR \\ I &= \frac{BLv}{R} \end{aligned}$$

parts.markscheme:
- $P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} I_{\text{max}}^2 R = \frac{1}{2} (0.16)^2 \cdot 5.0 = 0.0128 \cdot 5.0 = 0.064\ \text{W}$  
- Correct formula <marks> 1 mark </marks> and final result <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the peak velocity
steps.content:
The rod moves with a velocity described by a sine function: $v(t) = 4.0 \sin(200t)\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. 

In this expression, the coefficient in front of the sine term represents the maximum or **peak velocity** ($v_{\text{max}}$). This is the highest speed the rod reaches during its oscillation.

$$v_{\text{max}} = 4.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate peak induced EMF
steps.content:
When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced. We use the formula for motional EMF:

$$\varepsilon = Bvl$$

To find the **peak EMF** ($\varepsilon_{\text{max}}$), we substitute the peak velocity we identified in the previous step:

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= B \cdot v_{\text{max}} \cdot l \\ \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= 0.25 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 0.80 \\ \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= 0.80\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate peak EMF

steps.title: Determine peak current
steps.content:
Since the circuit is closed and has a total resistance $R$, we can find the **peak current** ($I_{\text{max}}$) using Ohm's Law ($V = IR$):

$$\begin{aligned} I_{\text{max}} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{max}}}{R} \\ I_{\text{max}} &= \frac{0.80}{5.0} \\ I_{\text{max}} &= 0.16\ \text{A} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate peak current

steps.title: Calculate average power
steps.content:
Because the current is sinusoidal, the power dissipated fluctuates over time. For a sinusoidal system, the **average power** ($P_{\text{avg}}$) is related to the peak current by the following relationship:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} I_{\text{max}}^2 R$$

This is derived from $P_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{rms}}^2 R$, where $I_{\text{rms}} = \frac{I_{\text{max}}}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{avg}} &= 0.5 \cdot (0.16)^2 \cdot 5.0 \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= 0.5 \cdot 0.0256 \cdot 5.0 \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= 0.064\ \text{W} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate average power

steps.title: Identify maximum velocity
steps.content:
To find the average power, we first need to determine the maximum (peak) conditions. Looking at the velocity function, we see it is a sine wave. The maximum value occurs when $\sin(200t) = 1$.

From the equation:
$$v(t) = 4.0 \sin(200t) \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

The peak velocity is:
$$v_{\text{max}} = 4.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate peak induced EMF
steps.content:
When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, an EMF is induced. Using the formula for motional EMF (from **Topic D.4: Induction**), we can find the peak voltage ($\varepsilon_{\text{max}}$):

$$\varepsilon_{\text{max}} = B L v_{\text{max}}$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= (0.25 \text{ T}) \cdot (0.80 \text{ m}) \cdot (4.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}) \\ \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= 0.80 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

This represents the maximum instantaneous voltage in the circuit.

steps.title: Apply average power formula
steps.content:
For a sinusoidal alternating current/voltage, the average power dissipated in a resistor is half of the peak power. Using the peak voltage ($\varepsilon_{\text{max}}$) and the total resistance ($R$), the formula is:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{max}}^2}{2R}$$

This relationship comes from the fact that $P_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}}$, and for sine waves, $V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}}$.

steps.title: Final power calculation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute our calculated peak EMF and the given resistance into the power equation:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{(0.80 \text{ V})^2}{2 \cdot (5.0 \Omega)} \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{0.64}{10.0} \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= 0.064 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, you receive **1 mark** for using the correct power relationship and **1 mark** for the correct final numerical value.

gdc.instructions: Enter the fraction (0.80^2) / (2 * 5.0) into the main calculation screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the average power using the peak voltage squared divided by twice the resistance.

steps.title: Identify peak velocity
steps.content:
To use the Peak Power Halving Method, we first need to find the maximum (peak) values. The velocity of the rod is given as $v(t) = 4.0 \sin(200t)\ \text{m s}^{-1}$. 

The maximum velocity occurs when the sine function is equal to 1:

$$v_{\text{max}} = 4.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate peak induced EMF
steps.content:
When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, the induced EMF ($\varepsilon$) is given by $\varepsilon = BvL$. Since the motion is perpendicular to the field, we can calculate the peak EMF ($\varepsilon_{\text{max}}$) using the peak velocity:

$$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= B \cdot v_{\text{max}} \cdot L \\ \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= 0.25 \cdot 4.0 \cdot 0.80 \\ \varepsilon_{\text{max}} &= 0.80\ \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find peak current
steps.content:
Next, we find the peak current ($I_{\text{max}}$) flowing through the circuit. Using Ohm's Law and the total resistance of the circuit:

$$\begin{aligned} I_{\text{max}} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{max}}}{R} \\ I_{\text{max}} &= \frac{0.80}{5.0} \\ I_{\text{max}} &= 0.16\ \text{A} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine peak power
steps.content:
The peak instantaneous power ($P_{\text{max}}$) occurs when both the EMF and current are at their maximum. We calculate this using the formula $P = I^2 R$:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{max}} &= I_{\text{max}}^2 \cdot R \\ P_{\text{max}} &= (0.16)^2 \cdot 5.0 \\ P_{\text{max}} &= 0.128\ \text{W} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for peak power.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation for peak current squared times resistance.

calculator.description: Calculate peak power using the peak current and resistance.

steps.title: Apply halving for average
steps.content:
For a purely resistive circuit where the current and voltage vary sinusoidally, the average power dissipated over a full cycle is exactly half of the peak power:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{2} P_{\text{max}} \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.128 \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= 0.064\ \text{W} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, one mark is awarded for using the correct relationship (incorporating the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$) and one mark for the final numerical result.

gdc.instructions: Find half of the peak power.

desmos.instructions: Divide the peak power result by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the final average power.

steps.title: Link force and velocity
steps.content:
To find the average power using the mechanical method, we first need to express the magnetic force $F$ acting on the rod. From the data booklet, the magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor is:

$$F = BIL\sin\theta$$

Since the rod moves perpendicular to the field, $\sin\theta = 1$. The induced current $I$ is caused by the motional EMF ($\varepsilon = BvL$) and is defined by Ohm's Law as $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = \frac{BvL}{R}$. Substituting this back into the force equation gives:

$$F = B\left(\frac{BvL}{R}\right)L = \frac{B^2L^2v}{R}$$

steps.title: Define instantaneous power
steps.content:
Mechanical power $P$ is the product of force and velocity ($P = Fv$). Substituting our expression for $F$ into this:

$$P(t) = \left(\frac{B^2L^2v(t)}{R}\right) \cdot v(t) = \frac{B^2L^2}{R} \cdot v(t)^2$$

Given that $v(t) = 4.0 \sin(200t)$, we can write the instantaneous power as:

$$P(t) = \frac{B^2L^2}{R} \cdot (4.0)^2 \sin^2(200t)$$ 

This shows that the power varies as a $\sin^2$ function over time.

steps.title: Determine average power
steps.content:
The average value of $\sin^2(\theta)$ over a full cycle is exactly $\frac{1}{2}$. Therefore, the average power $P_{\text{avg}}$ is half of the peak (maximum) power:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B^2 L^2 v_0^2}{R}$$

Where $v_0 = 4.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ is the maximum velocity. This matches the standard AC power relationship where $P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}I_0^2R$ because $I_0 = \frac{Bv_0L}{R}$.

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values:
- $B = 0.25\ \text{T}$
- $L = 0.80\ \text{m}$
- $v_0 = 4.0\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
- $R = 5.0\ \Omega$

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(0.25)^2 \cdot (0.80)^2 \cdot (4.0)^2}{5.0} \\ P_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{0.0625 \cdot 0.64 \cdot 16.0}{5.0} \end{aligned}$$

Performing the calculation:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = 0.064\ \text{W}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the run-matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the average power value using the formula derived.

parts.content: Explain how energy is conserved in this system.
parts.markscheme:
- Mechanical energy from the rod’s motion is converted into electrical energy <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- This electrical energy is dissipated as thermal energy in the resistor, maintaining conservation of energy <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the energy source
steps.content:
First, consider the motion of the conducting rod. As the rod moves through the magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force that opposes its motion (Lenz's Law). To keep the rod moving, work must be done against this force.

This represents the input of **mechanical energy** from the rod's motion.

steps.title: Conversion to electrical energy
steps.content:
According to the principles of electromagnetic induction (**Topic D.4**), the motion of the rod in the magnetic field induces an electromotive force (emf). This emf drives a current through the closed circuit.

Here, the mechanical energy is converted into **electrical energy**.

steps.title: Conversion to thermal energy
steps.content:
The circuit contains a resistor with resistance $R = 5.0\,\Omega$. As the induced current flows through this resistance, the electrical energy is dissipated as heat.

Here, the electrical energy is converted into **thermal energy**.

steps.title: State conservation of energy
steps.content:
To satisfy the law of conservation of energy (**Topic A.3**), the total energy in the system remains constant. The rate at which mechanical work is done to move the rod is exactly equal to the rate at which thermal energy is dissipated by the resistor.

**Summary of conversion:**
$$\text{Mechanical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Electrical Energy} \rightarrow \text{Thermal Energy}$$

steps.title: Identify the opposing magnetic force
steps.content:
As the rod moves through the magnetic field, an induced current flows. According to **Lenz's Law**, this current creates a magnetic force that opposes the motion of the rod. We can calculate the magnitude of this force using the equation for a current-carrying conductor:

$$F = BIL\sin\theta$$

steps.title: Explain mechanical work done
steps.content:
To maintain the rod's motion, an external force must act on the rod to overcome this opposing magnetic force. This requires **mechanical work** to be done on the system.

The work done is transferred from the mechanical energy of the rod's motion (or the external agent moving it) into the system.

steps.title: Describe energy transformation
steps.content:
This mechanical work done against the magnetic force is converted into **electrical energy**, driving the induced current through the circuit.

Finally, as the current flows through the resistor, this electrical energy is dissipated as **thermal energy** (heat).

steps.title: State conservation principle
steps.content:
Energy is conserved because the rate of mechanical work done against the magnetic force is exactly equal to the rate at which thermal energy is dissipated in the resistor.

$$\text{Mechanical Work Input} = \text{Electrical Energy} = \text{Thermal Energy Output}$$

questions.specification:
The square loop is rotating clockwise in a uniform magnetic field (into the page), with the axis of rotation in the plane of the loop and through its center (dashed line in the image provided).

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ciQ61VTJHc7XDf1_63hyN.jpeg)
